likely_responses.response: Motion to Dismiss — No Emergency Exists
likely_responses.argument: Defendant will argue current parenting arrangement is stable and no emergency justifies modifying it.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Child has been denied ALL parenting time with father since [date]. Complete denial of parenting time IS the emergency per MCL 722.27a(7). The status quo is the emergency.
likely_responses.authority: MCL 722.27a(7); Shade v Wright 291 Mich App 17 (2010)

likely_responses.argument: Defendant will assert child safety justifies zero parenting time, referencing PPO history.
likely_responses.rebuttal: PPO was obtained through fraud (documented false allegations). No evidence of actual harm. PPO has expired. Father has no criminal history of violence. Best interest factors favor contact per MCL 722.23.
likely_responses.authority: MCL 722.23; Vodvarka v Grasmeyer 259 Mich App 499 (2003)
likely_responses.risk: High — judge may defer to prior PPO narrative

likely_responses.argument: Defendant may ask court to appoint guardian ad litem or order psychological evaluation before changing custody.
likely_responses.rebuttal: While evaluation is appropriate for custody modification, it should not delay emergency restoration of parenting time. Court can order evaluation concurrent with supervised parenting time.
likely_responses.risk: Low — actually benefits father if evaluation is fair

likely_responses.response: Motion to Dismiss — Res Judicata / Collateral Estoppel
likely_responses.argument: These issues were already litigated in the custody case.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Fraud upon the court is NEVER barred by res judicata. MCR 2.612(C)(3) provides independent action for fraud with NO time limit. Hazel Park v Highland Park 350 Mich 286.
likely_responses.authority: MCR 2.612(C)(3); Hazel Park v Highland Park 350 Mich 286

likely_responses.argument: General denial — no fraud occurred, filings were truthful.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Documented contradictions between defendant's sworn statements and objective evidence (text messages, third-party witnesses, photos with metadata). Contradictions speak for themselves.

likely_responses.argument: Plaintiff is filing frivolous claims to harass defendant.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Michigan does not have an anti-SLAPP statute. Claims are supported by evidence. Pro se litigants entitled to liberal construction. Diehl v Danuloff 242 Mich App 120.
likely_responses.authority: Diehl v Danuloff 242 Mich App 120; MCR 2.114

likely_responses.response: Judge Refuses Recusal — No Bias Shown
likely_responses.argument: Prior rulings alone do not establish bias. Judge will claim impartiality.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Pattern of 1,127 documented violations goes far beyond routine rulings. Ex parte communications, denial of due process, failure to follow mandatory statutes. Armstrong v Ypsilanti 248 Mich App 573.
likely_responses.authority: MCR 2.003(C)(1); Armstrong v Ypsilanti 248 Mich App 573

likely_responses.response: Claim Waiver — Should Have Raised Earlier
likely_responses.argument: Failure to timely move for disqualification waives the right.
likely_responses.rebuttal: MCR 2.003(D)(1) requires motion within 14 days of discovering bias. New violations continue. Pattern only became clear through comprehensive analysis. Continuing violations doctrine applies.
likely_responses.authority: MCR 2.003(D)(1); In re Contempt of Dougherty 429 Mich 81

likely_responses.argument: Federal court should abstain from interfering with state family court proceedings.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Sprint Communications v Jacobs 571 US 69 — Younger is limited to 3 narrow categories. Bad faith prosecution / patently unconstitutional statutes exceptions apply when due process is denied.
likely_responses.authority: Sprint v Jacobs 571 US 69; Middlesex County Ethics v Garden State Bar 457 US 423
likely_responses.risk: High — most common federal defense in family law cases

likely_responses.argument: Federal courts lack jurisdiction over custody disputes.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Ankenbrandt v Richards 504 US 689 — exception is NARROW (divorce, alimony, custody decrees). §1983 constitutional claims are NOT custody disputes. Catz v Chalker 142 F.3d 279 (6th Cir).
likely_responses.authority: Ankenbrandt v Richards 504 US 689; Catz v Chalker 142 F.3d 279

likely_responses.argument: Judge McNeill has absolute judicial immunity from §1983 damages.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Dennis v Sparks 449 US 24 — conspiracy with private parties pierces immunity. Also seeking injunctive relief (Pulliam v Allen 466 US 522) which is not barred by immunity.
likely_responses.authority: Dennis v Sparks 449 US 24; Pulliam v Allen 466 US 522
likely_responses.risk: Medium — conspiracy allegation must be well-pled

likely_responses.argument: Cannot use federal court as appeals court for state court decisions.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Exxon Mobil v Saudi Basic Industries 544 US 280 — R-F is narrow, only bars "state-court losers" from appealing. Independent §1983 claims for constitutional violations are not appeals of state rulings.
likely_responses.authority: Exxon Mobil v Saudi Basic 544 US 280; Lance v Dennis 546 US 459

likely_responses.argument: MSC original jurisdiction requires extraordinary circumstances. Normal appellate process is adequate.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Void orders, systematic denial of due process, and judicial misconduct ARE extraordinary. COA process is inadequate when the entire lower court system is compromised.
likely_responses.authority: Const 1963 Art 6 §4; In re Hague 412 Mich 532
likely_responses.risk: High — MSC grants original jurisdiction rarely

likely_responses.argument: Rulings adverse to complainant do not constitute misconduct.
likely_responses.rebuttal: 1,127 documented violations, pattern of ex parte communications, denial of statutory rights. This goes far beyond adverse rulings — it is systematic misconduct.

likely_responses.response: No Proper Cause / Change of Circumstances
likely_responses.argument: Under Vodvarka, plaintiff must show proper cause or change of circumstances to modify custody.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Complete denial of ALL parenting time is a drastic change. Discovery of fraud in original proceedings is proper cause. Vodvarka v Grasmeyer 259 Mich App 499.
likely_responses.authority: Vodvarka v Grasmeyer 259 Mich App 499; MCL 722.27(1)(c)

likely_responses.argument: Child is established in current environment. Disruption not in best interest.
likely_responses.rebuttal: MCL 722.23 factor (j) — willingness to facilitate close relationship with other parent. Mother has completely eliminated father-child contact. Status quo IS the harm.
Application must demonstrate clear legal errors, not just disagreement with outcome. Focus on void orders (MCR 2.612) and constitutional violations.

likely_responses.argument: Trial court did not abuse discretion. Findings supported by evidence.
likely_responses.rebuttal: Abuse of discretion standard met when court ignores mandatory statutory factors, denies due process, and relies on fraudulent evidence. Not a close call.
likely_responses.authority: MCL 722.28; Berger v Berger 277 Mich App 700

likely_responses.response: Dismiss — Insufficient Evidence of Rule Violation
likely_responses.argument: Attorney exercised professional judgment. Withdrawal was proper.
likely_responses.rebuttal: MRPC 3.3 duty of candor requires attorney to correct false evidence presented to tribunal. Failure to correct known perjury is independent misconduct.
likely_responses.risk: Medium — AGC investigates if pattern shown
